summary: Joe Reis responds to a reader accusation that he has a low opinion of Europe by explaining that while he writes US-centric content due to his location and audience, he actually spends extensive time in Europe and often praises European data practices. He argues that different geographies have different tradeoffs—the US excels at wealth creation while Europe offers better quality of life and often higher data craftsmanship.
key_points: Geographic context shapes content, but global perspective informs worldview
Income does not equal competence—European data practices often show higher craftsmanship
US business culture prioritizes speed and quarterly numbers over long-term quality
Every geography has tradeoffs between wealth creation and quality of life
European data modeling and attention to quality often exceeds US standards
money_quotes: Does income = competence? Absolutely not. I've praised European data practices and often comment that I think Europe often does data 'better' than the US.
There's just a higher sense of craftsmanship and data competence in Europe. Perhaps it's because businesses move slower there. Maybe there's more time to think.
As someone told me in Paris last year, 'The US innovates and the EU regulates.'
In the US, making money is all about moving fast and being as 'productive' as possible to hit your quarterly number. We're not a long-term-oriented business culture.
Scott Galloway says the US is a great place to make money, and Europe is where you should spend it. That's my plan.
key_insight: Different geographies optimize for different outcomes—the US prioritizes wealth and speed while Europe emphasizes craftsmanship and quality of life—and neither approach is inherently superior.
